questions.q: What did the two armies do before the first arrow flew at Kurukshetra?
options: Exchanged hostages as surety of good faith
Swore a mutual code of rules for fair combat
Performed a joint sacrifice to Shiva together
Agreed to limit the war to ten days
questions.explanation: Kauravas and Pandavas together swore a covenant of battle conduct before the fighting began.

questions.q: At what moment was each day's fighting supposed to cease under the code?
options: At sunset
At midnight
At noon
At the fall of a commander
questions.explanation: Combat was to end at sundown each day, a rule shattered by the night battle of the fourteenth day.

questions.q: Which of these acts did the code of war expressly forbid?
options: Challenging a far stronger opponent
Fighting from the back of an elephant
Striking a warrior who had surrendered
Sounding conches before a charge
questions.explanation: Those who surrendered, retreated, or stood unarmed were not to be struck under the sworn code.

questions.q: Under the rule of equals, a chariot warrior was to duel whom?
options: Any soldier he happened to choose
The nearest elephant rider in the line
A foot soldier armed with a spear
Another warrior mounted on a chariot
questions.explanation: Equals were to fight equals: chariot against chariot, elephant against elephant, horseman against horseman.

questions.q: In mace combat, where was a blow strictly forbidden to land?
options: On the head
Below the navel
On the back
Above the shoulders
questions.explanation: Striking below the navel was the great taboo of mace-fighting, the very blow Bhima dealt Duryodhana.

questions.q: Which young warrior died when six maharathis attacked him together, shattering the code?
options: Abhimanyu
Ghatotkacha
Uttara
Iravan
questions.explanation: Sixteen-year-old Abhimanyu was surrounded and slain by six great warriors inside the Chakravyuha on the thirteenth day.

questions.q: In what state was Drona when he was beheaded, against every rule of combat?
options: Charging with his bow fully drawn
Asleep inside his tent at night
Unarmed and seated in meditation
Wounded and calling out for water
questions.explanation: Believing his son dead, Drona laid down his weapons and sat in meditation, and was killed in that defenseless state.

questions.q: What was Karna doing when Arjuna's arrow took his life?
options: Stringing his great bow Vijaya
Aiming the Vasavi Shakti dart
Blowing his conch in triumph
Unarmed, freeing his sunken chariot wheel
questions.explanation: Karna was struck down while he stood on the ground struggling to lift his chariot wheel from the earth.

questions.q: How did the code say a retreating or fleeing warrior must be treated?
options: He could be chased for one arrow's flight
He was not to be struck or pursued
He forfeited his weapons to the victor
He could be captured and held for ransom
questions.explanation: A warrior who quit the fight or turned away was protected from attack under the sworn rules.

questions.q: On which night did the armies battle on long after sunset, breaking the code outright?
options: The eighth night
The tenth night
The fourteenth night
The seventeenth night
questions.explanation: After Jayadratha's death the fighting raged through the fourteenth night, the night Ghatotkacha fell.

questions.q: Which battlefield figures did the code place beyond the reach of weapons?
options: Standard bearers and flag carriers
Kings commanding from the rear lines
Physicians alone, within the camps
Charioteers, drummers, and conch-blowers
questions.explanation: Non-combatants such as drivers, musicians, and bearers of supplies were never to be struck.

questions.q: What did the code demand of a warrior who saw two enemies already dueling?
options: He must not strike a man engaged with another
He could join in if his own side was losing
He must announce himself, then join freely
He should aim only at the stronger duelist
questions.explanation: One engaged in single combat with an opponent was not to be attacked by any third warrior.

questions.q: How were warriors who fought with words alone to be answered, under the code?
options: With a formal challenge to a duel
With banishment from the battlefield
With words only, never with weapons
With an appeal to the two commanders
questions.explanation: The covenant declared that those who contend with speech should be met with speech, not arms.

questions.q: From behind whom did Arjuna shoot the arrows that felled Bhishma on the tenth day?
options: Dhrishtadyumna
Shikhandi
Krishna
Yudhishthira
questions.explanation: Arjuna fought from behind Shikhandi, knowing the grandsire would not loose arrows in that direction.

questions.q: Why would Bhishma not raise his weapons against Shikhandi?
options: Shikhandi was his own grandnephew
Shikhandi fought entirely without armor
He had promised Drupada to show mercy
He refused to fight one who was born a woman
questions.explanation: Bhishma's personal code forbade fighting one born female, and Shikhandi was Amba reborn and raised as a son.

questions.q: Inside the Chakravyuha, what rule-breaking act did Karna commit against Abhimanyu?
options: He attacked from behind and severed Abhimanyu's bow
He struck Abhimanyu's unarmored back with a dart
He crushed Abhimanyu beneath his chariot wheels
He seized Abhimanyu's hair and dragged him down
questions.explanation: On Drona's counsel Karna cut the boy's bow from behind, after which others slew his horses and charioteer.

questions.q: What half-truth broke Drona's will to fight on the fifteenth day?
options: Krishna's claim that Hastinapura had fallen
Bhima's boast that Duryodhana lay dying
Yudhishthira's words that Ashwatthama was dead, the elephant murmured low
Sanjaya's report that the Kaurava camp was burning
questions.explanation: Bhima had killed an elephant named Ashwatthama, and Yudhishthira confirmed the death while muttering the qualifier.

questions.q: Who beheaded the unarmed, meditating Drona?
options: Bhima
Dhrishtadyumna
Shikhandi
Satyaki
questions.explanation: Dhrishtadyumna, born from fire to destroy Drona, took the guru's head as he sat with weapons laid down.

questions.q: How did Krishna signal Bhima during the final mace duel with Duryodhana?
options: He blew the Panchajanya twice
He pointed toward the setting sun
He whispered to Arjuna to call out
He slapped his own thigh
questions.explanation: Krishna struck his thigh in Bhima's sight, reminding him of his vow to break Duryodhana's thighs.

questions.q: Who erupted in fury, calling Bhima's low blow a disgrace to the laws of mace-fighting?
options: Balarama
Kripa
Ashwatthama
Yudhishthira
questions.explanation: Balarama, teacher of both mace fighters, raged that Bhima had struck below the navel against all rules.

questions.q: How did Bhurishravas die in violation of the warrior's code?
options: Trampled by war elephants while disarmed
Shot in the back while withdrawing from battle
Beheaded by Satyaki as he sat weaponless in meditation
Stabbed in his sleep during the night raid
questions.explanation: After Arjuna severed his sword arm mid-duel, itself a breach, Satyaki slew the seated warrior who had renounced the fight.

questions.q: When Abhimanyu's weapons were exhausted inside the wheel formation, what did he fight with at the last?
options: A torn battle standard
A chariot wheel lifted as weapon and shield
His dead charioteer's long whip
A fallen elephant's broken tusk
questions.explanation: Stripped of bow and sword, Abhimanyu raised a chariot wheel and fought on until he was finally brought down.

questions.q: When the trapped Karna invoked the rules of fair combat, how did Krishna reply?
options: He offered Karna safe passage if he yielded
He granted a pause until the wheel was freed
He recited the dharma Karna had broken at the dice hall and after
He declared that war itself acknowledges no rules
questions.explanation: Krishna asked where Karna's dharma had been when Draupadi was dragged to court and Abhimanyu was mobbed.

questions.q: How did the armies keep fighting after darkness fell on the fourteenth day?
options: Thousands of lamps and torches were lit across the field
The moon rose full and bright as day
Agni himself illumined the battlefield
The burning camps lit the whole plain
questions.explanation: The Drona Parva describes lamps fixed on chariots and elephants and torches in soldiers' hands turning night to day.

questions.q: Who delivered the final mace blow to the exhausted, weaponless Abhimanyu?
options: Karna himself
The son of Dushasana
Jayadratha at the gate
Shakuni's son Uluka
questions.explanation: Dushasana's son struck the fallen boy's head with a mace, completing the war's most infamous breach of the code.

questions.q: Ashwatthama's night raid trampled which most basic protection of the warrior's code?
options: That brahmins must never be made targets
That fire must not be used against camps
That commanders may die only in duels
That the sleeping and unarmed must never be slain
questions.explanation: The massacre of sleeping warriors after the war's end was the final and most complete violation of the covenant.

questions.q: What changed about Yudhishthira's chariot the moment he spoke his half-lie about Ashwatthama?
options: Its wheels, which had floated above the earth, sank to the ground
Its horses bolted in terror toward the Kaurava lines
Its banner of twin drums burst suddenly into flame
Its axle cracked beneath him in mid-battle
questions.explanation: The chariot that had always ridden above the earth by the king's truthfulness touched the ground for the first time.

questions.q: As he lay dying, Duryodhana enumerated the war's broken rules and accused whom of engineering them all?
options: Yudhishthira
Bhima
Krishna
Dhrishtadyumna
questions.explanation: Duryodhana charged Krishna with every unfair stratagem, and Krishna answered by recounting the Kauravas' own crimes.

questions.q: What trick drew Jayadratha out of hiding before Arjuna's sunset deadline expired?
options: A false report of Arjuna's funeral pyre
Krishna's discus veiled the sun, feigning dusk
Bhima's open challenge to single combat
A decoy chariot flying Arjuna's monkey banner
questions.explanation: When the sky darkened the Kauravas celebrated and Jayadratha emerged, only for the sun to blaze out again.

questions.q: After denouncing the thigh blow at the mace duel, what did the umpiring Balarama do?
options: He struck Bhima a single blow with his plough
He carried the broken Duryodhana from the field
He demanded the duel be fought once again
He left for Dwarka, declaring Duryodhana would win lasting glory
questions.explanation: Pacified by Krishna after raising his plough in anger, Balarama departed proclaiming Duryodhana's eternal fame as the fair fighter.
